From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Billboard chart

**R&B/Hip-Hop Airplay** (previously known as **Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Airplay**) is a chart published by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* magazine that ranks the top [R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B") and [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") songs in the United States, based on audience impressions from a panel of radio stations monitored by [Nielsen Broadcast Data Systems](/wiki/Nielsen_Broadcast_Data_Systems "Nielsen Broadcast Data Systems"). It was also used in sister publication *[R&R](/wiki/R%26R_(magazine) "R&R (magazine)")*, which listed the chart as Urban National Airplay. The chart is not the R&B/hip-hop subset of the [Hot 100 Airplay](/wiki/Hot_100_Airplay "Hot 100 Airplay") chart, but rather uses a separate panel of R&B stations in [urban](/wiki/Urban_contemporary "Urban contemporary") and [urban adult](/wiki/Urban_adult_contemporary "Urban adult contemporary") contemporary markets. It was the primary airplay component chart of the US [Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs](/wiki/Hot_R%26B/Hip-Hop_Songs "Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs") chart until the issue dated October 20, 2012, when Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs was revamped to include digital sales, streaming, and airplay from all radio formats.[[1]](#cite_note-Pietroluongo-1) The Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Airplay chart encompasses two separate airplay charts, both of which are based on radio spins rather than audience impressions: [Mainstream R&B/Hip-Hop](/wiki/Mainstream_R%26B/Hip-Hop "Mainstream R&B/Hip-Hop") and [Adult R&B Airplay](/wiki/Adult_R%26B_Songs "Adult R&B Songs"), which measure airplay on urban contemporary and urban adult contemporary stations respectively.

## Chart criteria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=R%26B/Hip-Hop_Airplay&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Chart criteria")]

There are fifty positions on the chart, which is solely based on radio airplay. 77 R&B and hip-hop radio stations are electronically monitored 24 hours a day, seven days a week by [Nielsen Broadcast Data Systems](/wiki/Nielsen_Broadcast_Data_Systems "Nielsen Broadcast Data Systems"). Songs are ranked by a calculation of the total number of spins per week with its audience impression, which is based upon exact times of airplay and each station's [Arbitron](/wiki/Arbitron "Arbitron") listener data.

Songs receiving the greatest growth will receive a "bullet", although there are tracks that will also get bullets if the loss in detections does not exceed the percentage of downtime from a monitored station. "Airpower" awards are issued to songs that appear on the top 20 of both the airplay and audience chart for the first time, while the "greatest gainer" award is given to song with the largest increase in detections. A song with six or more spins in its first week is awarded an "airplay add". If a song is tied for the most spins in the same week, the one with the biggest increase that previous week will rank higher, but if both songs show the same amount of spins regardless of detection the song that is being played at more stations is ranked higher. Songs that fall below the top 20 and have been on the chart after 26 weeks are removed and go to recurrent status.

## Other records and notable achievements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=R%26B/Hip-Hop_Airplay&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Other records and notable achievements")]

* "[Adorn](/wiki/Adorn_(song) "Adorn (song)")" by [Miguel](/wiki/Miguel_(singer) "Miguel (singer)") was the first song to spend at least 20 weeks atop the chart. *Billboard* reported that the track was able to do so because the R&B/Hip-Hop Airplay chart reporting panel is composed of a variety of mainstream and adult R&B radio stations. Skip Dillard, the operations manager at adult R&B [WBLS](/wiki/WBLS "WBLS") New York, stated that Miguel appeals to listeners in both the 25-34 and 35-54 age groups, thus maximizing the reach of his audience.[[35]](#cite_note-35) "Adorn" went on to spend a total of 23 weeks at number-one, which was, at that point, a record.[[10]](#cite_note-Adorn_23_weeks-10)
* [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé") holds the record for the most number-ones among female acts, with ten. [Alicia Keys](/wiki/Alicia_Keys "Alicia Keys") follows with eight, [Brandy](/wiki/Brandy_Norwood "Brandy Norwood") follows with five, and [Aaliyah](/wiki/Aaliyah "Aaliyah"), [Erykah Badu](/wiki/Erykah_Badu "Erykah Badu") and [Mariah Carey](/wiki/Mariah_Carey "Mariah Carey"), all three of whom have attained four.[[18]](#cite_note-Beyonce_Scores_Sixth_No._1_On_R&B/Hip-Hop_Airplay_Chart-18)
* The 1993 [single](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)") "[That's the Way Love Goes](/wiki/That%27s_the_Way_Love_Goes_(Janet_Jackson_song) "That's the Way Love Goes (Janet Jackson song)")" by American singer [Janet Jackson](/wiki/Janet_Jackson "Janet Jackson") holds the record for being the only song in the history of the chart to debut at number one, which did so in May 1993.[[36]](#cite_note-36)
